name: Histories of Indian Perfume
description:
Scent carries memory. 
The smell of ripe mangoes transports you to childhood summers. Rose attar takes you to a Nawab’s palace you never visited. 
This exhibition traces the histories of perfume in India through texts, paintings, records, and objects left behind. Map how scent entered cuisine, medicine, worship. Follow trade routes that carried oils and essences across continents. Discover who made perfume, how it was distilled, where labor and craft intersect with commerce and ritual. 
In collaboration with MARG Magazine, the exhibition culminates in a conversation with James McHugh, Giti Datt, and Ahalya Matthan on Sat, Feb 28, 11:30am. Link to the session here. 
Image and text. Scent and history. 
Walkthroughs: 
Tue, Feb 24 – 4 PM
Thu, Feb 26 – 4 PM
Sat, Feb 28 – 1 PM

name: Dance With Space
description:
Five decades inside a form leaves its knowledge in the body. This evening, Malavika Sarukkai brings it to you. 
One of India’s foremost Bharatanatyam artistes, Sarukkai presents a selection of her choreographies alongside the creative process that shaped them, the layering of intention and intuition, the place where classical grammar meets a contemporary mind. Accompanied by four musicians, she moves between demonstration and reflection, making visible what usually remains privy to the artist alone. 
The evening is open to all: rasikas, dancers, scholars, and enthusiasts alike. 
Photo credits: Mayur Channagere 
Managed by Shreya Nagarajan Singh Arts Development Consultancy. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Entangled Worlds
description:
Science has never been apolitical. It has just been silent about politics and social identities. 
This talk traces the entanglements between nature and society across thirty years of field research in India and Latin America, moving between blackbuck conservation in Maharashtra, iguana habitats in Costa Rica, fisheries in the Brazilian Amazon, Afro-Colombian movements, and pastoralist struggles in Kutch. 
What connects them is not geography but a single multiple entanglements of culture, nature, and political economy. 
A Q&A will follow the lecture.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: First-Hand
description:
First-hand. First, Hand. 
An experience that begins with the hand, with touch. The most direct sense through which we connect with the world. 
Blindfolded, headphones on, you’re guided through forests, seas, and desert plains for 90 minutes. The world arrives through your fingertips: unexpected textures and sensations that reorient you. Your hands doing the work your eyes usually do. Through evocative storytelling, you traverse sensory worlds shaped by the intelligence and wonder of the animal kingdom. 
An invitation to attune yourself differently. To recalibrate away from the visual tyranny of screens and return to your body’s older, quieter ways of knowing the natural world. 
Please Note: Limited to 10 participants per session.

name: Abhijñānaśākuntalam: The Circle of Love
description:
Kālidāsa never met Kūṭiyāṭṭam. Until now. 
Nepathya presents a scene from Abhijñānaśākuntalam, Kālidāsa’s most celebrated play: the moment when Duṣyanta and Śakuntalā meet and fall in love. Conceived and designed by Guru Margi Madhu Chakyar, this adaptation brings together what history kept apart. 
In this adaptation, the Sūta enacts Duṣyanta’s actions. Duṣyanta mirrors the deer. His responses echo Śakuntalā’s movements. What emerges is emotions synchronizing across bodies, a complementary interplay that grew from attempting the impossible. 
Kūṭiyāṭṭam stretches time. A gesture can last minutes. A glance becomes an entire emotional landscape. What you see is intensity, immediacy, and a particular kind of attention. 
Kālidāsa. Kūṭiyāṭṭam. They meet at last. 
Part of Pravāha 2026, The Body As Space: A Journey Through The Senses. This year’s festival is built around what we hear, smell, touch, taste, and see. Eight days of multisensory experiences through classical music, contemporary dance, installations, and conversations. 
Presented by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Living Authentically
description:
Courage is not the beginning of self-knowledge. It is the constant condition of it. 
Courage to Be You follows two people asking that question from very different lives. Amit and Najib are a gay couple navigating not only their relationship but the religious and social distance between them. Rajeshwari, Amit’s childhood friend, is quietly working to reclaim an identity fractured by early trauma. Their stories run parallel, but their questions are the same: what does it take to choose yourself, and what do you risk when you do? 
Rashna Imhasly-Gandhy draws on Jungian psychology and chakra healing, offering the reader a framework for the same inner work her characters undertake. Their guide, in both cases, is a therapist. Their destination is their own. 
Yasmeen Premji will introduce the session. Rashna’s presentation will be followed by a conversation between her and Malini White.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Pravāha 2026
description:
Born from a vision to unite masters and experimentalists on a single stage, Pravāha debuted in 2024. The second edition of this festival, The Body As Space, explores how we experience the world through sight, sound, touch, taste, and smell. 
Over eight days, the festival becomes a journey through sensation. Classical melodies meet olfactory landscapes. Contemporary dance transforms into tactile dialogue. Installations immerse you in sound, scent, and memory itself. Conversations uncover India’s forgotten sensory archives and trace the origins of instruments that changed how we listen. 
We invite you to tune into what you’ve always carried: the intelligence of your body, the stories held in your senses. 
RSVP for the events you’d like to attend here.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Ultimate Secrets of Wild Poop
description:
Poop is a bad word. It is also, depending on who you ask, a home, a weapon, a meal, and a way to save the planet. 
In the animal kingdom, dung is serious business. Herbivores use scat the way we use social media. Tigers use it to mark territory. Dung beetles build homes in it and eat it for breakfast. Hoopoes weaponise it. Blue whales poop in quantities that actually help keep our oceans alive. And scientists collect all of it, enthusiastically, in the name of conservation. 
Join author Shweta Taneja for a noisy, messy, gloriously disgusting science session on wild animals and what they leave behind. 
Warning: you will be grossed out, you will giggle, and you will learn things you cannot unlearn. 

Around the World in 50 Sundays is a journey that begins, not with a ticket, but with opening a book. In a city like Bangalore where families are always in motion, the BIC Library hopes to offer stillness and a quiet, welcoming space for both parents and children where reading becomes a shared way of being and not a task. 
Every Sunday, we open our doors to invite you into a library that listens; building a rhythm of return, a habit of joy and a new community bound not just by books, but by the weekly ritual of reading together. 

Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Beyond Accompaniment
description:
The harmonium has spent centuries being useful. 
It has held the shruti, supported the vocalist, filled the space between phrases with something steady and unobtrusive. It has been, in the best possible sense, reliable. What it has rarely been given is the stage to itself. 
This recital does exactly that. The programme traverses khayal, thumri, tappa, dhun, theatre music, and devotional compositions, moving between gayaki ang and tantrakari ang, the vocal and instrumental traditions. One segment demonstrates how a shift in genre changes the technique, the phrasing, the entire interpretive logic of the instrument. The kind of detail that makes you realise you have never quite listened to it before. 
The harmonium, it turns out, has been waiting. And this is its debut. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Design, Skills, and Transcontinental Flair
description:
For over forty years, legendary British designer Dame Zandra Rhodes and Indian design visionary Rajeev Sethi have shared a rare creative partnership rooted in friendship, curiosity, and cross-cultural exchange. From the landmark Festival of India exhibitions to collaborations with artisans across the subcontinent, their work has reimagined how traditional craft, contemporary design, and global aesthetics can meet. 
In this special conversation, Rhodes and Sethi reflect on journeys through India’s textile traditions, experiments that bridged craft and couture, and the communities of makers whose skills shaped their work. They will discuss the evolving role of design in sustaining cultural heritage, the transformative power of collaboration, and the personal bond that has sustained their creative dialogue across decades. 
Rich with stories of travel, experimentation, and shared vision, this session offers a rare glimpse into a friendship that helped reshape global conversations around craft, fashion, and cultural diplomacy. 
  
Image Credits
All images in the posters and header are from early photographs of Zandra Rhodes and Rajeev Sethi preparing for the Festival of India in 1981.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Colors of Hindustani Slide Guitar
description:
The slide guitar was not born into Hindustani classical music. Joel ‘Veena’ Eisenkramer has spent a lifetime bringing it home. 
Eisenkramer has spent decades inside the Hindustani oral tradition, in conversation with musicians, collecting the stories and lore that live between the notes. This evening, he brings all of it to the stage: traditional ragas and original compositions, interwoven with stories told as they were meant to be, spoken and spontaneous. Anand HS accompanies on tabla. 
An evening that asks something of the listener, and returns considerably more. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: The Cities We Carry
description:
Two questions plagued her for five years, living between Delhi, Venice, and Istanbul: how do we lose what we lose, and why do we love whom we love? 
Drawing on her collection of essays Place: Intimate Encounters with Cities (2026), written across 25 years and 13 cities, Ananya Vajpeyi will explore what cities hold for us.  The memory and meaning held in street corners and corridors of light; the way personal loss and political trouble have of finding each other in the very same spaces. 
Architect Prem Chandavarkar brings his own lifelong reckoning with cities to the table. Between them, the conversation promises to move across continents, disciplines, and the question of what it means to love a place. 
The conversation will be followed by a Q&A with the audience.
